html:
XII

I was summoned to the study when Lawrence Redding arrived. He looked haggard, and, I thought, suspicious. Colonel Melchett greeted him with something approaching cordiality.

вАЬWe want to ask you a few questionsвБ†вАФhere, on the spot,вАЭ he said.

Lawrence sneered slightly.

вАЬIsnвАЩt that a French idea? Reconstruction of the crime?вАЭ

вАЬMy dear boy,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett, вАЬdonвАЩt take that tone with us. Are you aware that someone else has also confessed to committing the crime which you pretend to have committed?вАЭ

The effect of these words on Lawrence was painful and immediate.

вАЬSвАСsвАСomeone else?вАЭ he stammered. вАЬWhoвБ†вАФwho?вАЭ

вАЬMrs.¬†Protheroe,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett, watching him.

вАЬAbsurd. She never did it. She couldnвАЩt have. ItвАЩs impossible.вАЭ

Melchett interrupted him.

вАЬStrangely enough, we did not believe her story. Neither, I may say, do we believe yours. Dr.¬†Haydock says positively that the murder could not have been committed at the time you say it was.вАЭ

вАЬDr.¬†Haydock says that?вАЭ

вАЬYes, so, you see, you are cleared whether you like it or not. And now we want you to help us, to tell us exactly what occurred.вАЭ

Lawrence still hesitated.

вАЬYouвАЩre not deceiving me aboutвБ†вАФabout Mrs.¬†Protheroe? You really donвАЩt suspect her?вАЭ

вАЬOn my word of honour,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett.

Lawrence drew a deep breath.

вАЬIвАЩve been a fool,вАЭ he said. вАЬAn absolute fool. How could I have thought for one minute that she did itвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬSuppose you tell us all about it?вАЭ suggested the Chief Constable.

вАЬThereвАЩs not much to tell. IвБ†вАФI met Mrs.¬†Protheroe that afternoonвБ†вАФвАЭ

He paused.

вАЬWe know all about that,вАЭ said Melchett. вАЬYou may think that your feeling for Mrs.¬†Protheroe and hers for you was a dead secret, but in reality it was known and commented upon. In any case, everything is bound to come out now.вАЭ

вАЬVery well, then. I expect you are right. I had promised the vicar here (he glanced at me) toвБ†вАФto go right away. I met Mrs.¬†Protheroe that evening in the studio at a quarter past six. I told her of what I had decided. She, too, agreed that it was the only thing to do. WeвБ†вАФwe said goodbye to each other.

вАЬWe left the studio, and almost at once Dr.¬†Stone joined us. Anne managed to seem marvellously natural. I couldnвАЩt do it. I went off with Stone to the Blue Boar and had a drink. Then I thought IвАЩd go home, but when I got to the corner of this road, I changed my mind and decided to come along and see the vicar. I felt I wanted someone to talk to about the matter.

вАЬAt the door, the maid told me the vicar was out, but would be in shortly, but that Colonel Protheroe was in the study waiting for him. Well, I didnвАЩt like to go away againвБ†вАФlooked as though I were shirking meeting him. So I said IвАЩd wait too, and I went into the study.вАЭ

He stopped.

вАЬWell?вАЭ said Colonel Melchett.

вАЬProtheroe was sitting at the writing tableвБ†вАФjust as you found him. I went up to himвБ†вАФtouched him. He was dead. Then I looked down and saw the pistol lying on the floor beside him. I picked it upвБ†вАФand at once saw that it was my pistol.

вАЬThat gave me a turn. My pistol! And then, straightaway I leaped to one conclusion. Anne must have bagged my pistol some time or otherвБ†вАФmeaning it for herself if she couldnвАЩt bear things any longer. Perhaps she had had it with her today. After we parted in the village she must have come back here andвБ†вАФandвБ†вАФoh! I suppose I was mad to think of it. But thatвАЩs what I thought. I slipped the pistol in my pocket and came away. Just outside the Vicarage gate, I met the vicar. He said something nice and normal about seeing ProtheroeвБ†вАФsuddenly I had a wild desire to laugh. His manner was so ordinary and everyday and there was I all strung up. I remember shouting out something absurd and seeing his face change. I was nearly off my head, I believe. I went walkingвБ†вАФwalkingвБ†вАФat last I couldnвАЩt bear it any longer. If Anne had done this ghastly thing, I was, at least, morally responsible. I went and gave myself up.вАЭ

There was a silence when he had finished. Then the colonel said in a businesslike voice:

вАЬI would like to ask just one or two questions. First, did you touch or move the body in any way?вАЭ

вАЬNo, I didnвАЩt touch it at all. One could see he was dead without touching him.вАЭ

вАЬDid you notice a note lying on the blotter half concealed by his body?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬDid you interfere in any way with the clock?вАЭ

вАЬI never touched the clock. I seem to remember a clock lying overturned on the table, but I never touched it.вАЭ

вАЬNow as to this pistol of yours, when did you last see it?вАЭ

Lawrence Redding reflected.

вАЬItвАЩs hard to say exactly.вАЭ

вАЬWhere do you keep it?вАЭ

вАЬOh, in a litter of odds and ends in the sitting-room in my cottage. On one of the shelves of the bookcase.вАЭ

вАЬYou left it lying about carelessly?вАЭ

вАЬYes. I really didnвАЩt think about it. It was just there.вАЭ

вАЬSo that anyone who came to your cottage could have seen it?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬAnd you donвАЩt remember when you last saw it?вАЭ

Lawrence drew his brows together in a frown of recollection.

вАЬIвАЩm almost sure it was there the day before yesterday. I remember pushing it aside to get an old pipe. I think it was the day before yesterdayвБ†вАФbut it may have been the day before that.вАЭ

вАЬWho has been to your cottage lately?вАЭ

вАЬOh! Crowds of people. Someone is always drifting in and out. I had a sort of tea party the day before yesterday. Lettice Protheroe, Dennis, and all their crowd. And then one or other of the old Pussies comes in now and again.вАЭ

вАЬDo you lock the cottage up when you go out?вАЭ

вАЬNo; why on earth should I? IвАЩve nothing to steal. And no one does lock their house up round here.вАЭ

вАЬWho looks after your wants there?вАЭ

вАЬAn old Mrs.¬†Archer comes in every morning to вАШdo for meвАЩ as itвАЩs called.вАЭ

вАЬDo you think she would remember when the pistol was there last?вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt know. She might. But I donвАЩt fancy conscientious dusting is her strong point.вАЭ

вАЬIt comes to thisвБ†вАФthat almost anyone might have taken that pistol?вАЭ

вАЬIt seems soвБ†вАФyes.вАЭ

The door opened and Dr.¬†Haydock came in with Anne Protheroe.

She started at seeing Lawrence. He, on his part, made a tentative step towards her.

вАЬForgive me, Anne,вАЭ he said. вАЬIt was abominable of me to think what I did.вАЭ

вАЬIвБ†вАФвАЭ She faltered, then looked appealingly at Colonel Melchett. вАЬIs it true, what Dr.¬†Haydock told me?вАЭ

вАЬThat Mr.¬†Redding is cleared of suspicion? Yes. And now what about this story of yours, Mrs.¬†Protheroe? Eh, what about it?вАЭ

She smiled rather shamefacedly.

вАЬI suppose you think it dreadful of me?вАЭ

вАЬWell, shall we sayвБ†вАФvery foolish? But thatвАЩs all over. What I want now, Mrs.¬†Protheroe, is the truthвБ†вАФthe absolute truth.вАЭ

She nodded gravely.

вАЬI will tell you. I suppose you know aboutвБ†вАФabout everything.вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬI was to meet LawrenceвБ†вАФMr.¬†ReddingвБ†вАФthat evening at the studio. At a quarter past six. My husband and I drove into the village together. I had some shopping to do. As we parted he mentioned casually that he was going to see the vicar. I couldnвАЩt get word to Lawrence, and I was rather uneasy. IвБ†вАФwell, it was awkward meeting him in the Vicarage garden whilst my husband was at the Vicarage.вАЭ

Her cheeks burned as she said this. It was not a pleasant moment for her.

вАЬI reflected that perhaps my husband would not stay very long. To find this out, I came along the back lane and into the garden. I hoped no one would see me, but of course old Miss Marple had to be in her garden! She stopped me and we said a few words, and I explained I was going to call for my husband. I felt I had to say something. I donвАЩt know whether she believed me or not. She looked ratherвБ†вАФfunny.

вАЬWhen I left her, I went straight across to the Vicarage and round the corner of the house to the study window. I crept up to it very softly, expecting to hear the sound of voices. But to my surprise there were none. I just glanced in, saw the room was empty, and hurried across the lawn and down to the studio where Lawrence joined me almost at once.вАЭ

вАЬYou say the room was empty, Mrs.¬†Protheroe?вАЭ

вАЬYes, my husband was not there.вАЭ

вАЬExtraordinary.вАЭ

вАЬYou mean, maвАЩam, that you didnвАЩt see him?вАЭ said the inspector.

вАЬNo, I didnвАЩt see him.вАЭ

Inspector Slack whispered to the Chief Constable, who nodded.

вАЬDo you mind, Mrs.¬†Protheroe, just showing us exactly what you did?вАЭ

вАЬNot at all.вАЭ

She rose, Inspector Slack pushed open the window for her, and she stepped out on the terrace and round the house to the left.

Inspector Slack beckoned me imperiously to go and sit at the writing-table.

Somehow I didnвАЩt much like doing it. It gave me an uncomfortable feeling. But, of course, I complied.

Presently I heard footsteps outside, they paused for a minute, then retreated. Inspector Slack indicated to me that I could return to the other side of the room. Mrs.¬†Protheroe reentered through the window.

вАЬIs that exactly how it was?вАЭ asked Colonel Melchett.

вАЬI think exactly.вАЭ

вАЬThen can you tell us, Mrs.¬†Protheroe, just exactly where the vicar was in the room when you looked in?вАЭ asked Inspector Slack.

вАЬThe vicar? IвБ†вАФno, IвАЩm afraid I canвАЩt. I didnвАЩt see him.вАЭ

Inspector Slack nodded.

вАЬThatвАЩs how you didnвАЩt see your husband. He was round the corner at the writing-desk.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ she paused. Suddenly her eyes grew round with horror. вАЬIt wasnвАЩt there thatвБ†вАФthatвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYes, Mrs.¬†Protheroe. It was while he was sitting there.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ She quivered.

He went on with his questions.

вАЬDid you know, Mrs.¬†Protheroe, that Mr.¬†Redding had a pistol?вАЭ

вАЬYes. He told me so once.вАЭ

вАЬDid you ever have that pistol in your possession?вАЭ

She shook her head.

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬDid you know where he kept it?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not sure. I thinkвБ†вАФyes. I think IвАЩve seen it on a shelf in his cottage. DidnвАЩt you keep it there, Lawrence?вАЭ

вАЬWhen was the last time you were at the cottage, Mrs.¬†Protheroe?вАЭ

вАЬOh! About three weeks ago. My husband and I had tea there with him.вАЭ

вАЬAnd you have not been there since?вАЭ

вАЬNo. I never went there. You see, it would probably cause a lot of talk in the village.вАЭ

вАЬDoubtless,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett dryly. вАЬWhere were you in the habit of seeing Mr.¬†Redding, if I may ask?вАЭ

She blushed.

вАЬHe used to come up to the Hall. He was painting Lettice. WeвБ†вАФwe often met in the woods afterwards.вАЭ

Colonel Melchett nodded.

вАЬIsnвАЩt that enough?вАЭ Her voice was suddenly broken. вАЬItвАЩs so awfulвБ†вАФhaving to tell you all these things. AndвБ†вАФand there wasnвАЩt anything wrong about it. There wasnвАЩtвБ†вАФindeed, there wasnвАЩt. We were just friends. WeвБ†вАФwe couldnвАЩt help caring for each other.вАЭ

She looked pleadingly at Dr.¬†Haydock, and that softhearted man stepped forward.

вАЬI really think, Melchett,вАЭ he said, вАЬthat Mrs.¬†Protheroe has had enough. SheвАЩs had a great shockвБ†вАФin more ways than one.вАЭ

The Chief Constable nodded.

вАЬThere is really nothing more I want to ask you, Mrs.¬†Protheroe,вАЭ he said. вАЬThank you for answering my questions so frankly.вАЭ

вАЬThenвБ†вАФthen I may go?вАЭ

вАЬIs your wife in?вАЭ asked Haydock. вАЬI think Mrs.¬†Protheroe would like to see her.вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ I said, вАЬGriselda is in. YouвАЩll find her in the drawing-room.вАЭ

She and Haydock left the room together and Lawrence Redding with them.

Colonel Melchett had pursed up his lips and was playing with a paper knife. Slack was looking at the note. It was then that I mentioned Miss MarpleвАЩs theory.

Slack looked closely at it.

вАЬMy word,вАЭ he said, вАЬI believe the old ladyвАЩs right. Look here, sir, donвАЩt you see?вБ†вАФthese figures are written in different ink. That date was written with a fountain pen or IвАЩll eat my boots!вАЭ

We were all rather excited.

вАЬYouвАЩve examined the note for fingerprints, of course,вАЭ said the Chief Constable.

вАЬWhat do you think, colonel? No fingerprints on the note at all. Fingerprints on the pistol those of Mr.¬†Lawrence Redding. May have been some others once, before he went fooling round with it and carrying it around in his pocket, but thereвАЩs nothing clear enough to get hold of now.вАЭ

вАЬAt first the case looked very black against Mrs.¬†Protheroe,вАЭ said the colonel thoughtfully. вАЬMuch blacker than against young Redding. There was that old woman MarpleвАЩs evidence that she didnвАЩt have the pistol with her, but these elderly ladies are often mistaken.вАЭ

I was silent, but I did not agree with him. I was quite sure that Anne Protheroe had had no pistol with her since Miss Marple had said so. Miss Marple is not the type of elderly lady who makes mistakes. She has got an uncanny knack of being always right.

вАЬWhat did get me was that nobody heard the shot. If it was fired thenвБ†вАФsomebody must have heard itвБ†вАФwherever they thought it came from. Slack, youвАЩd better have a word with the maid.вАЭ

Inspector Slack moved with alacrity towards the door.

вАЬI shouldnвАЩt ask her if she heard a shot in the house,вАЭ I said. вАЬBecause if you do, sheвАЩll deny it. Call it a shot in the wood. ThatвАЩs the only kind of shot sheвАЩll admit to hearing.вАЭ

вАЬI know how to manage them,вАЭ said Inspector Slack, and disappeared.

вАЬMiss Marple says she heard a shot later,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett thoughtfully. вАЬWe must see if she can fix the time at all precisely. Of course it may be a stray shot that had nothing to do with the case.вАЭ

вАЬIt may be, of course,вАЭ I agreed.

The colonel took a turn or two up and down the room.

вАЬDo you know, Clement,вАЭ he said suddenly, вАЬIвАЩve a feeling that this is going to turn out a much more intricate and difficult business than any of us think. Dash it all, thereвАЩs something behind it.вАЭ He snorted. вАЬSomething we donвАЩt know about. WeвАЩre only beginning, Clement. Mark my words, weвАЩre only beginning. All these things, the clock, the note, the pistolвБ†вАФthey donвАЩt make sense as they stand.вАЭ

I shook my head. They certainly didnвАЩt.

вАЬBut IвАЩm going to get to the bottom of it. No calling in of Scotland Yard. SlackвАЩs a smart man. HeвАЩs a very smart man. HeвАЩs a kind of ferret. HeвАЩll nose his way through to the truth. HeвАЩs done several very good things already, and this case will be his chef dвАЩoeuvre. Some men would call in Scotland Yard. I shanвАЩt. WeвАЩll get to the bottom of this here in Downshire.вАЭ

вАЬI hope so, IвАЩm sure,вАЭ I said.

I tried to make my voice enthusiastic, but I had already taken such a dislike to Inspector Slack that the prospect of his success failed to appeal to me. A successful Slack would, I thought, be even more odious than a baffled one.

вАЬWho has the house next?вАЭ asked the Colonel suddenly.

вАЬYou mean at the end of the road? Mrs.¬†Price Ridley.вАЭ

вАЬWeвАЩll go along to her after Slack has finished with your maid. She might just possibly have heard something. She isnвАЩt deaf or anything, is she?вАЭ

вАЬI should say her hearing is remarkably keen. IвАЩm going by the amount of scandal she has started by вАШjust happening to overhear accidentally.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs the kind of woman we want. Oh! hereвАЩs Slack.вАЭ

The inspector had the air of one emerging from a severe tussle. He looked hot.

вАЬPhew!вАЭ he said. вАЬThatвАЩs a tartar youвАЩve got, sir.вАЭ

вАЬMary is essentially a girl of strong character,вАЭ I replied.

вАЬDoesnвАЩt like the police,вАЭ he said. вАЬI cautioned herвБ†вАФdid what I could to put the fear of the law into her, but no good. She stood right up to me.вАЭ

вАЬSpirited,вАЭ I said, feeling more kindly towards Mary.

вАЬBut I pinned her down all right. She heard one shotвБ†вАФand one shot only. And it was a good long time after Colonel Protheroe came. I couldnвАЩt get her to name a time, but we fixed it at last by means of the fish. The fish was late, and she blew the boy up when he came, and he said it was barely half-past six anyway, and it was just after that she heard the shot. Of course, thatвАЩs not accurate, so to speak, but it gives us an idea.вАЭ

вАЬHвАЩm,вАЭ said Melchett.

вАЬI donвАЩt think Mrs.¬†ProtheroeвАЩs in this after all,вАЭ said Slack, with a note of regret in his voice. вАЬShe wouldnвАЩt have had time, to begin with, and then women never like fiddling about with firearms. ArsenicвАЩs more in their line. No, I donвАЩt think she did it. ItвАЩs a pity!вАЭ

He sighed.

Melchett explained that he was going round to Mrs.¬†Price RidleyвАЩs, and Slack approved.

вАЬMay I come with you?вАЭ I asked. вАЬIвАЩm getting interested.вАЭ

I was given permission, and we set forth. A loud вАЬHieвАЭ greeted us as we emerged from the Vicarage gate, and my nephew, Dennis, came running up the road from the village to join us.

вАЬLook here,вАЭ he said to the inspector, вАЬwhat about that footprint I told you about?вАЭ

вАЬGardenerвАЩs,вАЭ said Inspector Slack laconically.

вАЬYou donвАЩt think it might be someone else wearing the gardenerвАЩs boots?вАЭ

вАЬNo, I donвАЩt!вАЭ said Inspector Slack in a discouraging way.

It would take more than that to discourage Dennis, however.

He held out a couple of burnt matches.

вАЬI found these by the Vicarage gate.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ said Slack, and put them in his pocket.

Matters appeared now to have reached a deadlock.

вАЬYouвАЩre not arresting Uncle Len, are you?вАЭ inquired Dennis facetiously.

вАЬWhy should I?вАЭ inquired Slack.

вАЬThereвАЩs a lot of evidence against him,вАЭ declared Dennis. вАЬYou ask Mary. Only the day before the murder he was wishing Colonel Protheroe out of the world. WerenвАЩt you, Uncle Len?вАЭ

вАЬErвБ†вАФвАЭ I began.

Inspector Slack turned a slow suspicious stare upon me, and I felt hot all over. Dennis is exceedingly tiresome. He ought to realize that a policeman seldom has a sense of humour.

вАЬDonвАЩt be absurd, Dennis,вАЭ I said irritably.

The innocent child opened his eyes in a stare of surprise.

вАЬI say, itвАЩs only a joke,вАЭ he said. вАЬUncle Len just said that anyone who murdered Colonel Protheroe would be doing the world a service.вАЭ

вАЬAh!вАЭ said Inspector Slack, вАЬthat explains something the maid said.вАЭ

Servants very seldom have any sense of humour either. I cursed Dennis heartily in my mind for bringing the matter up. That and the clock together will make the inspector suspicious of me for life.

вАЬCome on, Clement,вАЭ said Colonel Melchett.

вАЬWhere are you going? Can I come, too?вАЭ asked Dennis.

вАЬNo, you canвАЩt,вАЭ I snapped.

We left him looking after us with a hurt expression. We went up to the neat front door of Mrs.¬†Price RidleyвАЩs house and the inspector knocked and rang in what I can only describe as an official manner.

A pretty parlourmaid answered the bell.

вАЬMrs.¬†Price Ridley in?вАЭ inquired Melchett.

вАЬNo, sir.вАЭ The maid paused and added: вАЬSheвАЩs just gone down to the police station.вАЭ

This was a totally unexpected development. As we retraced our steps Melchett caught me by the arm and murmured:

вАЬIf sheвАЩs gone to confess to the crime, too, I really shall go off my head.вАЭ
